summary: This cluster of research investigates the adaptive strategies and ecological roles of marine macroalgae and microalgae under environmental stress, with a particular focus on delayed development in microscopic life stages. Key species examined include *Ascophyllum nodosum*, *Porphyra pulchella*, and the harmful dinoflagellate *Cochlodinium polykrikoides*, alongside the diatom *Porosira pseudodenticulata*, which together illustrate diverse morphological and molecular responses to challenging conditions. The studies emphasize taxonomic relationships within the *Scytosiphonaceae* and *Phaeophyceae* families, integrating molecular data to clarify phylogenetic connections across geographically distinct marine ecosystems. Findings collectively highlight how cryptic life-history phases and stress-induced dormancy enable these algae to persist and distribute across broad geographic ranges, underscoring their resilience in dynamic coastal environments.
